evidence.scientific_clarity: Grouped bar chart clearly shows OursModel leads on all 5 benchmarks; error bars, hatches, and bold legend entry make the winner immediately obvious at single-column width.
evidence.typography: pdffonts shows DejaVuSans and DejaVuSans-Bold — the default matplotlib font family, not a publication-quality face like Helvetica/Arial/Source Sans; rcParams sets fonttype=42 so text is editable, but the font choice itself is the default DejaVu.
evidence.palette: Uses the Wong colorblind-safe palette (#999999 grey, #56B4E9 sky-blue, #E69F00 orange, #D55E00 vermilion); no red/green directional misuse; hatches added for B&W readers.
evidence.layout_density: Figure is 3.5×2.6 in; y-range computed dynamically from data min/max so headroom is tight; no obvious wasted space or overlapping labels; tight_layout applied.
evidence.reviewer_readiness: Caption matches the figure and includes a specific quantitative claim (3.3–5.2 pp margin); legend is 2-col upper-left and does not obscure bars; minor quibble: no explicit units label on x-axis and legend header is missing, but no fatal issues.
evidence.vector_fidelity: PDF exists and is 44 KB (non-trivial); fonttype=42 is set in rcParams so text should be selectable, but pdffonts confirms DejaVu CID TrueType fonts are embedded — text is editable vector, not rasterized.
evidence.file_format: figure.pdf (44 KB), figure.png (82 KB), figure.svg (176 KB), caption.tex (309 B), and gen_figure.py (3.1 KB) all present and non-trivial.
evidence.caption_quality: Caption names all five benchmarks, explains error bars (5 seeds std), identifies the winning method, and gives a precise numerical advantage (3.3–5.2 percentage points).

overall_strengths: Colorblind-safe Wong palette with hatching ensures accessibility for all readers including B&W printing.
Caption is publication-ready: identifies the winner by name, names all benchmarks, explains error bars, and provides a specific quantitative claim.
overall_weaknesses: Font family defaults to DejaVu Sans (matplotlib default) instead of a publication-standard face such as Helvetica, Arial, or Source Sans Pro, which would likely prompt a typography comment from reviewers.
Legend lacks a header and the x-axis has no explicit benchmark-group label, which are minor but common reviewer complaints in top-venue submissions.
